question: [File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take()] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the market's loan token charges a fee-on-transfer (e.g., 1% fee) trigger the call sequence take() -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets), violating the invariant that contract token balances must cover withdrawable assets and claimableSettlementFee (claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += buyerAssets - sellerAssets is recorded at full face value but only (buyerAssets - sellerAssets) * (1 - fee) arrives), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where claimableSettlementFee and withdrawable are overcounted relative to actual token balance, leading to insolvency on subsequent withdraw() or claimSettlementFee() calls? Proof idea: deploy a fee-on-transfer ERC20 as loanToken, execute take() with nonzero settlement fee, then assert midnight.claimableSettlementFee(loanToken) > IERC20(loanToken).balanceOf(address(midnight)) after the take.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: repay()] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence repay(market, units, onBehalf, address(0), '') -> position[id][onBehalf].debt -= units -> marketState[id].withdrawable += units -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, msg.sender, address(this), units), violating the invariant that withdrawable must not exceed actual token balance (withdrawable is incremented by full units but only units*(1-fee) arrives), causing scoped impact: insolvency where lenders calling withdraw() will drain the contract balance before all credit is redeemed? Proof idea: fuzz fee percentage and repay amount; assert sum(withdrawable across all markets for loanToken) <= IERC20(loanToken).balanceOf(address(midnight)) after repay.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: supplyCollateral()] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where a collateral token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf) -> position[id][onBehalf].collateral[collateralIndex] += assets -> safeTransferFrom(collateralToken, msg.sender, address(this), assets), violating the invariant that position.collateral[i] must not exceed actual token balance held by the protocol (position records full assets but only assets*(1-fee) arrives), causing scoped impact: bad debt creation where health check uses inflated collateral value allowing undercollateralized borrowing via subsequent take()? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer collateral, supplyCollateral(1000), assert position.collateral[0] == 1000 but IERC20(collateral).balanceOf(midnight) == 990, then verify take() succeeds for debt that would be unhealthy at true collateral value.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: withdraw()] Can an unprivileged lender under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) -> position.credit -= units -> marketState.withdrawable -= units -> marketState.totalUnits -= units -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units), violating the invariant that receiver must receive exactly units of loanToken (receiver gets units*(1-fee) but credit is fully decremented), causing scoped impact: fund freeze where lender loses fee amount on every withdrawal with no protocol recourse, and repeated withdrawals drain the contract balance faster than accounting tracks? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer loanToken, lend N units, withdraw N units, assert receiver balance == N*(1-fee) and midnight loanToken balance decreased by N not N*(1-fee).

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence liquidate() -> _position.debt -= repaidUnits -> marketState.withdrawable += repaidUnits -> safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets) -> onLiquidate callback -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), repaidUnits), violating the invariant that withdrawable must not exceed actual loanToken balance (withdrawable is incremented by full repaidUnits but only repaidUnits*(1-fee) arrives), causing scoped impact: insolvency where lenders cannot fully redeem credit after liquidation because withdrawable exceeds actual balance? Proof idea: fuzz repaidUnits and fee rate; assert marketState.withdrawable <= IERC20(loanToken).balanceOf(midnight) after liquidation.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator under preconditions where the collateral token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence liquidate() -> position.collateral[collateralIndex] -= seizedAssets -> safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets), violating the invariant that the liquidator must receive seizedAssets of collateral (receiver gets seizedAssets*(1-fee) but position is decremented by full seizedAssets), causing scoped impact: direct loss to liquidator who receives less collateral than the debt they repaid implies, potentially making liquidation unprofitable and leaving unhealthy positions unliquidated? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer collateral, create unhealthy position, liquidate, assert receiver collateral balance < seizedAssets and verify liquidation profit is negative after fee.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: flashLoan()] Can an unprivileged callback receiver under preconditions where a flash-loaned token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence flashLoan(tokens, assets, callback, data) -> safeTransfer(tokens[i], callback, assets[i]) -> onFlashLoan callback -> safeTransferFrom(tokens[i], callback, address(this), assets[i]), violating the invariant that flashLoan must be self-contained with no net balance change (callback receives assets[i]*(1-fee) but must repay full assets[i], making repayment impossible unless callback has extra balance), causing scoped impact: fund freeze where flashLoan always reverts for fee-on-transfer tokens, permanently blocking flash liquidation and arbitrage flows that rely on flashLoan? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer token, call flashLoan with that token, assert revert on safeTransferFrom repayment step.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: claimSettlementFee()] Can an unprivileged fee claimer under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) -> claimableSettlementFee[token] -= amount -> safeTransfer(token, receiver, amount), violating the invariant that claimableSettlementFee[token] must not exceed actual token balance (claimableSettlementFee is decremented by full amount but receiver gets amount*(1-fee), and the protocol's balance decreases by amount not amount*(1-fee)), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where repeated fee claims drain the contract balance faster than claimableSettlementFee tracks, eventually causing lender withdrawals to revert? Proof idea: set up market with fee-on-transfer loanToken, accumulate settlement fees via take(), claim fees, assert IERC20(loanToken).balanceOf(midnight) < sum of all withdrawable + remaining claimableSettlementFee.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: claimContinuousFee()] Can an unprivileged fee claimer under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) -> continuousFeeCredit -= amount -> totalUnits -= amount -> withdrawable -= amount -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, amount), violating the invariant that withdrawable must equal actual redeemable loanToken balance (withdrawable is decremented by full amount but only amount*(1-fee) leaves the contract, so the contract retains amount*fee extra but withdrawable is already reduced), causing scoped impact: accounting desync where the contract holds more tokens than withdrawable tracks, but those tokens are unclaimable (stuck), effectively freezing fee-on-transfer residuals? Proof idea: fuzz continuous fee accrual and claim amount; assert IERC20(loanToken).balanceOf(midnight) - withdrawable[id] == accumulated_fee_residuals after claim.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: withdrawCollateral()] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where the collateral token charges a fee-on-transfer trigger the call sequence withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf, receiver) -> position.collateral[collateralIndex] -= assets -> health check -> safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, assets), violating the invariant that receiver must receive exactly assets of collateral (receiver gets assets*(1-fee) but position is fully decremented), causing scoped impact: direct loss to borrower who receives less collateral than withdrawn, with no protocol mechanism to recover the fee amount which is permanently stuck in the contract? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer collateral, supplyCollateral(1000), withdrawCollateral(1000), assert receiver balance == 990 and midnight collateral balance == 10 (stuck).

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer() / safeTransferFrom()] Can an unprivileged market creator under preconditions where the loan token returns false on transfer (ERC20False pattern) trigger the call sequence withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) -> returndata decodes to false -> revert(TransferReturnedFalse), violating the invariant that lenders must be able to exit the market (withdraw must not permanently revert for valid credit holders), causing scoped impact: fund freeze where all lender withdrawals are permanently blocked if the loan token's transfer() returns false, even though the token safety requirements only exclude tokens that revert on no-op transfers? Proof idea: deploy ERC20False as loanToken, lend units, attempt withdraw, assert revert with TransferReturnedFalse selector; verify no recovery path exists.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom()] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the loan token returns false on transferFrom trigger the call sequence take() -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> returndata decodes to false -> revert(TransferFromReturnedFalse), violating the invariant that take() must succeed for valid offers with sufficient payer balance (take reverts permanently for tokens that return false on transferFrom), causing scoped impact: fund freeze where all take() calls are permanently blocked for a market using a token that conditionally returns false (e.g., after a blacklist or pause), freezing all lender and borrower activity? Proof idea: deploy ERC20 that returns false on transferFrom after a flag is set, execute take(), set flag, attempt take() again, assert permanent revert.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer() / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator under preconditions where the collateral token returns false on transfer trigger the call sequence liquidate() -> safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, seizedAssets) -> returndata decodes to false -> revert(TransferReturnedFalse), violating the invariant that unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable (liquidate() permanently reverts for collateral tokens that return false on transfer), causing scoped impact: liquidation failure where unhealthy positions accumulate bad debt that can never be realized, eventually making the market insolvent? Proof idea: deploy ERC20False as collateral, create unhealthy position, attempt liquidate(), assert revert; verify bad debt cannot be socialized.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: SafeTransferLib / token with no code] Can an unprivileged market creator under preconditions where the loan token self-destructs after market creation trigger the call sequence withdraw(market, units, onBehalf, receiver) -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) -> require(token.code.length > 0, NoCode()) -> revert(NoCode), violating the invariant that lenders must be able to exit the market (withdraw permanently reverts when loanToken has no code), causing scoped impact: permanent fund freeze for all lenders in the market, with no recovery path since market params are immutable after creation? Proof idea: create market with self-destructable loanToken, lend units, self-destruct token, attempt withdraw, assert revert with NoCode selector.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the buyerCallback (IBuyCallback) reverts trigger the call sequence take() -> position/state updates committed to storage -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) -> IBuyCallback.onBuy() -> revert -> entire take() reverts, violating the invariant that LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT transient storage must be cleared on revert (EVM reverts tstore changes on revert, so lock is cleared), but if the revert is caught by an outer try/catch in MidnightBundles or a custom router, the transient lock for seller persists for the rest of the transaction, causing scoped impact: liquidation failure where seller cannot be liquidated during the remainder of the transaction even if unhealthy? Proof idea: wrap take() in try/catch, have onBuy revert, assert liquidationLocked(id, seller) == true after the caught revert within the same transaction.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the sellerCallback (ISellCallback) returns a value other than CALLBACK_SUCCESS trigger the call sequence take() -> position updates -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, this, buyerAssets-sellerAssets) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) -> ISellCallback.onSell() returns bytes32(0) -> require(...== CALLBACK_SUCCESS) fails -> revert(WrongSellCallbackReturnValue), violating the invariant that token transfers already executed must be rolled back on revert (they are, since EVM reverts the whole tx), but verifying that the LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT is also correctly cleared on this revert path, causing scoped impact: if lock is not cleared (e.g., caught externally), seller is protected from liquidation for the rest of the transaction? Proof idea: deploy ISellCallback returning bytes32(0), wrap take() in try/catch, assert liquidationLocked(id, seller) after caught revert.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: repay() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where the onRepay callback reenters repay() for the same borrower trigger the call sequence repay(market, units, onBehalf, callback, data) -> position.debt -= units -> withdrawable += units -> IRepayCallback.onRepay() -> [reentrant] repay(market, units, onBehalf, address(0), '') -> position.debt -= units again -> withdrawable += units again -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, msg.sender, this, units) [inner] -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, units) [outer], violating the invariant that debt reduction must correspond to exactly one token transfer per unit (debt is decremented twice but only one transfer is forced by the outer call since inner call uses msg.sender not callback), causing scoped impact: bad debt creation where borrower's debt is reduced by 2*units but only units tokens are pulled? Proof idea: deploy reentrant onRepay callback, assert position.debt after == position.debt before - 2*units but only units tokens transferred.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: repay() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where the onRepay callback reenters withdraw() for a lender trigger the call sequence repay(market, units, onBehalf, callback, data) -> position[borrower].debt -= units -> marketState.withdrawable += units -> IRepayCallback.onRepay() -> [reentrant] withdraw(market, units, lender, receiver) -> position[lender].credit -= units -> marketState.withdrawable -= units -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, units) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, this, units) [outer repay], violating the invariant that withdrawable must remain non-negative and token balance must cover all claims (lender withdraws units that were just made withdrawable by repay, then repay pulls units again from callback, net effect: protocol receives 2*units but lender already withdrew units), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where withdrawable is manipulated to allow double-counting? Proof idea: stateful fuzz with reentrant onRepay that calls withdraw; assert withdrawable conservation.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: liquidate() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged liquidator under preconditions where the onLiquidate callback reenters liquidate() for the same borrower trigger the call sequence liquidate(market, collateralIndex, 0, repaidUnits, borrower, false, receiver, callback, data) -> bad debt realized -> position.debt -= repaidUnits -> withdrawable += repaidUnits -> safeTransfer(collateral, receiver, seizedAssets) -> ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate() -> [reentrant] liquidate(market, collateralIndex, 0, repaidUnits2, borrower, false, receiver, address(0), '') -> LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT check: liquidationLocked(id, borrower) is false (lock is only set in take(), not liquidate()), violating the invariant that a single liquidation call must not allow double-liquidation of the same position within one transaction, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure where borrower's collateral is seized twice in one transaction? Proof idea: deploy reentrant onLiquidate, assert position.collateral[collateralIndex] after == 0 and repaidUnits pulled == 2*repaidUnits.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: flashLoan() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS] Can an unprivileged callback receiver under preconditions where the onFlashLoan callback reenters flashLoan() with the same tokens trigger the call sequence flashLoan(tokens, assets, callback, data) -> safeTransfer(tokens[0], callback, assets[0]) -> IFlashLoanCallback.onFlashLoan() -> [reentrant] flashLoan(tokens, assets, callback, data) -> safeTransfer(tokens[0], callback, assets[0]) [inner, but midnight may not have enough balance] -> inner onFlashLoan -> safeTransferFrom(tokens[0], callback, this, assets[0]) [inner repay] -> safeTransferFrom(tokens[0], callback, this, assets[0]) [outer repay], violating the invariant that flashLoan must not allow net token extraction (if inner flashLoan succeeds and outer repayment also succeeds, callback ends up with 0 net but protocol balance is unchanged; but if inner fails mid-way, outer repayment may still succeed leaving protocol short), causing scoped impact: insolvency via reentrancy in flashLoan? Proof idea: deploy reentrant flashLoan callback, assert IERC20(token).balanceOf(midnight) is unchanged after nested flashLoan.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take() / CALLBACK_SUCCESS / LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the onBuy callback reenters take() with the same seller trigger the call sequence take(offer1) -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) [wasLocked=false] -> IBuyCallback.onBuy() -> [reentrant] take(offer2, same seller) -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) [wasLocked=true, so inner take does NOT clear lock] -> inner take completes -> outer take completes -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, false) [clears lock], violating the invariant that the seller's debt must not increase after maturity (if inner take increases seller debt post-maturity, the maturity check applies per-call but the lock state may mask the health check), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where seller accumulates debt across two nested takes without a health check between them? Proof idea: fuzz nested take with same seller, assert seller health after both takes complete.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take() / ERC777-like hooks] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the loan token implements ERC777-like tokensReceived hooks trigger the call sequence take() -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets-sellerAssets) -> token.tokensReceived hook fires on Midnight contract -> reentrant call into Midnight (e.g., withdraw(), repay(), liquidate()), violating the invariant that callbacks must not corrupt partial state through reentrancy (the TOKEN SAFETY REQUIREMENTS in Midnight.sol explicitly require tokens must not re-enter Midnight on transfer), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where reentrancy via token hook observes and exploits partially-updated state (position updates are committed before safeTransferFrom)? Proof idea: deploy ERC777-like loanToken with tokensReceived hook that calls withdraw(); assert credit/withdrawable invariants after take().

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: supplyCollateral() / ERC777-like hooks] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where the collateral token implements ERC777-like tokensToSend hooks trigger the call sequence supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf) -> position.collateral[collateralIndex] += assets -> bitmap updated -> safeTransferFrom(collateralToken, msg.sender, address(this), assets) -> token.tokensToSend hook fires on msg.sender -> reentrant call into Midnight (e.g., withdrawCollateral()), violating the invariant that collateral bitmap/list state must match actual deposited collateral (bitmap is set before transfer; if reentrant withdrawCollateral() fires before transfer completes, bitmap may be in inconsistent state), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where collateral bitmap shows active collateral that was never actually deposited? Proof idea: deploy ERC777-like collateral with tokensToSend hook calling withdrawCollateral(); assert bitmap vs actual balance consistency.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: take() / CBP = 1e12 / settlement fee precision] Can an unprivileged market creator under preconditions where the settlement fee is set to a value between 0 and CBP (1e12) trigger the call sequence setDefaultSettlementFee(loanToken, index, fee) where fee < CBP -> settlementFeeCbp stored as uint16 = fee/CBP = 0 -> take() -> settlementFee() returns 0 -> claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += 0, violating the invariant that settlement fees must be collected as configured (fees below 1 cbp = 1e12 WAD units are silently rounded to zero), causing scoped impact: fee undercollection where a market creator can set fees just below CBP precision to avoid all settlement fees, depriving the protocol of revenue? Proof idea: set settlementFee to CBP-1, execute take(), assert claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] == 0.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: maxLif() / LLTV_8 = 1e18 / liquidate()] Can an unprivileged market creator under preconditions where LLTV = LLTV_8 = 1e18 (WAD) trigger the call sequence touchMarket(market with lltv=WAD) -> maxLif(WAD, LIQUIDATION_CURSOR_LOW) = mulDivDown(WAD, WAD, WAD - mulDivDown(0.25e18, WAD-WAD, WAD)) = mulDivDown(WAD, WAD, WAD) = WAD -> maxLif = WAD -> liquidate() -> lif = WAD -> seizedAssets = repaidUnits.mulDivDown(WAD, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, price) = repaidUnits/price, violating the invariant that liquidators must receive a profit incentive (LIF=1 means no incentive, so rational liquidators will not liquidate LLTV=1 markets), causing scoped impact: liquidation failure where unhealthy LLTV=1 positions accumulate bad debt that is never liquidated, eventually making the market insolvent? Proof idea: create LLTV=1 market, create unhealthy position, verify maxLif==WAD, simulate liquidation profit == 0, assert no rational liquidator would call liquidate().
